strings: NOTE: Selection depends on application, capacity, maintenance requirements, and site conditions.
Low temperature e.g., air, water, ground
Read the map at your duty point
At your duty point, read capacity and power from the corresponding contours.
Use the side factors to understand how changes affect performance.
Higher displacement shifts capacity and power upward (proportionally).
Higher efficiency shifts capacity upward (more capacity for given conditions).
Higher condensing temperature or lower evaporating temperature increases pressure ratio (moves up and left).
Discharge temperature increases toward the upper-left of the map (higher condensing temperature and/or lower evaporating temperature).
Performance map at the duty point
Oil is entrained in discharge gas as an oil mist.
The oil separator captures oil and returns it to the compressor.
Oil return line (capillary) must be sized and routed properly.
Oil hold-up in the evaporator increases liquid slug risk on suction.
Piston moves up and down in a cylinder. Suction on the downstroke, compression on the upstroke.
Two spiral scrolls orbit. Gas is trapped in pockets that move inward and are compressed continuously.
Two intermeshing helical rotors trap and compress gas as it moves axially through the compressor.
A high-speed impeller throws gas outward. Velocity is converted to pressure in the diffuser.
An eccentrically mounted rotor turns in a circular housing. Gas is trapped in chambers and compressed.
Unit controller / PLC — I/O and interlocks
ANY TRIP OPENS CHAIN -> UNIT SHUTDOWN
Safety chain is wired in series.
If any device opens, outputs are de-energized.
Events, alarms, and trends stored locally and in cloud (if connected).
RELIABLE CONTROL. SAFE OPERATION. MAXIMUM UPTIME.
Collects real-time data and executes local control & safety interlocks.
Aggregates, normalizes and securely publishes data to the cloud.
Turns data into insights and recommended actions.
Operators review performance, receive alerts and approve actions.
Commands are validated and applied by the local controller within limits.
Feedback loop: updated setpoints & model learning
Performance feedback improves models and future recommendations.
Safety first: The local controller always enforces hard safety limits and interlocks. Remote commands cannot override hard safety.
Electrical layers in a heat-pump package
Motor and compressor share a sealed shell. Windings are bathed in refrigerant-oil mixture for cooling and lubrication.
No shaft seal — no leakage path
Replace as a unit (not field serviceable)
Motor is outside the compressor. Power is transmitted through a shaft with a seal.
Shaft seal is a wear item — requires maintenance
Efficiency can be higher (motor optimised independently)
Both need correct insulation class and start method.
Main protection against short circuit and overload
Remotely switches power to the load
Steps down to safe control voltage
Power (high current) separated from control (low voltage)
Set overload to nameplate FLC — do not raise to stop trips.
Reduces conducted emissions to the grid.
IGBT bridge generates variable frequency 3-phase AC
Wide speed range for capacity control
Harmonics reduced on output with sine-wave filtering
EMI filters limit interference to meet standards
Protections: overcurrent, overvoltage, overtemp, ground fault, short circuit, phase loss
The VFD adjusts compressor speed to match load — reducing energy use while maintaining precise superheat and system stability.
Bolted Frame, Folded Sheet-Metal Panels, Hinged Access Door
Structural support made from bolted steel frame for strength and stability.
Folded sheet-metal panels provide enclosure and weather protection.
Hinged access door allows service access to internal components.
IP54 protection against limited dust ingress and splashing water from any direction.
The steel frame provides structural support.
The door allows access for service.
The ground connection bonds the enclosure to earth for safety.
Enclosure roles in a heat-pump package
CABLE TRAY ROUTING INSIDE HEAT PUMP ENCLOSURE
Proper cable routing improves reliability and serviceability. Follow minimum bend radius and secure cables at recommended tie points.
Maintain minimum bend radius. R ≥ 10 x cable OD
Use cable tray or ladder for mechanical protection
Secure cables at regular intervals using tie points
Maintain minimum bend radius to prevent damage
Provide strain relief at all panel entries
Keep power and signal cables separated as required
Follow all applicable codes and manufacturer instructions
Keep power and signal circuits separate to improve EMC performance and reduce noise coupling.
Signal / sensor cables in separate tray
Physical separation between power and signal circuits
Cable shield connected to chassis at entry to drain high-frequency noise
Separate power and signal cables in different trays or compartments.
Maintain adequate distance between power and signal paths.
Terminate cable shields 360° to chassis at panel entry.
Avoid running cables above variable speed drives and switching devices.
Boundaries overlap by brand, fluid, and duty — confirm with OEM software.
Clean Oil In → Clean System → Reliable Operation
Use oil specified by equipment manufacturer
Store sealed in clean, dry area
Evacuate system to required vacuum level
Charge oil through low point using clean, dry equipment
Break vacuum with dry refrigerant only
Maintain proper oil level in compressor
Ensure good oil circulation and return
May lead to ice or sludge
Can plug oil passages and strainers
Contaminants degrade oil and harm equipment
Check oil condition at regular intervals
Identify and correct the source of contamination
Use properly sized filter-drier to remove moisture and acid
Change oil if degraded, contaminated, or after component failure
Evacuate system and recharge with clean oil
Record oil type, charge amount, and condition
Clean oil protects equipment, improves efficiency, and extends system life.
Oil lubricates bearings, pistons, and other moving parts.
Oil mist is removed from the discharge gas. Clean refrigerant continues to the condenser.
Metered oil return via capillary tube back to compressor crankcase.
A thin oil film remains on internal tube surfaces, returning to the compressor with refrigerant.
Refrigerant is compressed. Oil is entrained in the discharge gas as mist.
Oil mist is removed in the separator. Clean refrigerant flows to the condenser.
Oil is metered through a capillary tube back to the compressor sump.
Refrigerant rejects heat in the condenser and condenses to liquid.
Liquid refrigerant is metered through the expansion device.
Refrigerant absorbs heat in the evaporator. Oil film returns to the compressor.
Oil path in the refrigeration cycle
Typical properties and refrigerant compatibility guide
Not recommended with most HFC or HFO
Not recommended with HCFC, HFO, ammonia or CO2
Some use with CO2 (with appropriate additives)
Some HFC systems (where miscibility is adequate)
Hygroscopic – keep system clean and sealed.
Additive package may be required for wear protection in CO2 systems.
Lubricant viscosity decreases as temperature increases.
The viscosity must stay within an optimal window for reliable lubrication across operating conditions.
High viscosity at low temperature for film thickness and wear protection.
Viscosity must remain above the lower limit to maintain lubrication and prevent wear.
A measure of a fluid's resistance to flow. Essential for forming a protective lubricant film between moving parts.
Reduces wear and extends component life
Supports reliable performance across wide temperature range
Higher viscosity – better film strength at high temperature
Lower viscosity – better low temperature fluidity
Select the appropriate ISO VG grade to ensure viscosity stays within the optimal window from cold start to high discharge temperature.
Steel or copper pipe provides structural strength.
Zinc coating (hot-dip) provides sacrificial corrosion protection.
Epoxy or fusion-bonded epoxy provides a barrier against moisture, chemicals, and abrasion.
Provides inherent corrosion resistance without the need for galvanizing or protective coatings in many environments.
The zinc layer corrodes preferentially to the base metal (sacrificial protection).
The outer coating acts as a barrier to prevent exposure to corrosive elements, extending the service life of the pipe.
Use traps if riser > 20 ft. (6 m)
At low points every 20-30 ft. (6-9 m)
Install vibration isolators near the compressor and at regular intervals.
Diagram shows heating mode. Refrigerant flow reverses in cooling mode.
Follow manufacturer recommendations and applicable codes.
Keep piping runs as short and direct as possible.
Minimize the number of elbows and fittings.
Use proper line sizing for capacity and oil return.
Pressure test and evacuate per standard practice.
Seal insulation and avoid heat gain.
Do not route near sleeping areas if possible.
Install filter drier and sight glass.
Protect from physical damage and heat sources.
Proper oil return is critical for compressor life.
Pitch suction line up toward compressor.
Use oil traps at low points.
For vertical risers > 20 ft. (6 m), use traps.
Ensure adequate velocity in suction line.
Refrigerant piping roles in the cycle
If the cold pipe surface temperature is below the dew point of the surrounding air, moisture will condense on the pipe.
Closed-cell foam reduces heat gain and prevents condensation.
Prevents moisture from entering the insulation.
REFRIGERANT PIPE SIZING AND SUPPORT SPACING
Refrigerant piping must be properly sized, supported, and sloped to ensure oil return and system reliability. Provide adequate support spacing and install oil traps at risers.
Slope horizontal piping in the direction of refrigerant flow.
Minimum slope: 1% (10 mm per meter) toward the flow direction.
Install oil traps at the base of risers greater than 6 m (20 ft).
Ensure all piping is securely supported and protected from vibration.
Follow manufacturer guidelines and applicable codes.
Secures the pipe to the support
Rubber isolator reduces vibration transmission to the structure
Prevents stress on the piping system
Proper support selection and installation help prevent vibration, reduce noise, and protect the piping system from stress caused by thermal expansion and contraction.
GWP landscape for heat pump fluids (schematic)
Legacy HFC (declining) — R410A, R134a, R245fa — GWP 675—4000+
Application temperature band → Comfort HP / Commercial / Industrial & HTHP
Not a property chart — confirm values in engineering datasheets
International policy framework for refrigerants & heat pumps
Montreal Protocol (1987) — ODS phase-out — CFC, HCFC
Fluid choice ← Montreal / Kigali + national F-gas law
Deployment scale ← Paris / electrification & grid decarbonisation
Only Montreal & Kigali directly regulate refrigerants; Kyoto & Paris drive heat pump adoption context
China · EU · United States — regulatory eras (schematic)
Three jurisdictions, three clocks — never assume one global ban date
*Generations overlap in time; treaty dates are orientation markers only.
Heat Pump Inline Production Test Bench
100% testing of every unit – Leak, Safety, Function
Refrigerant circuit pressurised with forming gas (N2)
Quality built in at every step. Test. Verify. Improve. Repeat.
Record results, maintain traceability, ensure accountability.
Review data, identify trends, and drive continuous improvement.
Address root causes, update standards, prevent recurrence.
Build skills, share best practices, standardize work.
Controlled source (evaporator) and sink (condenser) conditions with instrumentation and performance monitoring
Essential tools for installation, commissioning and service
Evacuates air and moisture from the refrigerant circuit to ensure efficient and reliable system operation.
Recovers, evacuates and charges refrigerant with precision for safe and accurate system commissioning.
Detects refrigerant leaks quickly and accurately to help ensure system integrity.
Applies the correct torque to flare nuts and fasteners to prevent leaks and ensure reliable connections.
Always follow manufacturer instructions and local regulations. Use appropriate PPE and safe handling practices.
4-way reversing valve · heating vs cooling paths
D connects to E, C connects to S
D connects to C, E connects to S
Stuck mid-stroke bypasses high to low and kills capacity.
TXV vs EEV — control of superheat
EEV is a control interface, not a more expensive TXV.
Thermostatic expansion - meters liquid / holds SH
Water-side actuators are separate — keep refrigerant and hydronic BOMs apart.
Prevents liquid refrigerant from reaching the compressor by separating and holding liquid at low velocity, allowing only vapor to pass to the compressor.
Low-temp evaporators with high load variation
Systems with long suction lines or floodback potential
Protects compressors from liquid slugging and damage
Only vapor passes through to the compressor.
Slows down incoming flow and directs it against the vessel wall to drop out liquid.
Liquid refrigerant collects here. Volume is sized to handle system surges and variations.
Oil carried with the vapor condenses in the accumulator and returns to the system through the U-bend or orifice pick-up.
Holds back liquid during normal operation and surges so only vapor reaches the compressor.
Oil returns to the compressor only when carried with vapor. Too much liquid hold-up can trap oil and cause oil starvation. Size the accumulator correctly.
Size for system capacity and surge
Insulate to prevent sweat and icing
Ensure oil return path is always open
Removes oil from hot discharge gas before the condenser.
Oil returns to the compressor via float, differential and check valve.
Prevents liquid slugging of the compressor.
Stores refrigerant during low load and defrost.
Keeps the compressor safe and stable.
Stores liquid refrigerant at high pressure.
Provides steady supply to the expansion device.
Ensures stable feed and system capacity.
Separates vapor and liquid at intermediate pressure.
Improves capacity and efficiency; reduces compressor work.
Supports efficiency and low compressor ratio.
Start diagnostics at oil return and liquid management.
Good oil return, proper subcooling and safe suction = reliable system.
Separates high-pressure liquid into vapor and liquid at an intermediate pressure for efficient multi-stage compression and high-lift (HTHP) systems.
To compressor mid-injection port or economizer port
High-pressure liquid enters and flashes to an intermediate pressure. Vapor separates from liquid.
Operating pressure is between condenser (high side) and evaporator (low side).
To lower expansion stage (EXV / TXV / Valve)
Maintains proper liquid level in the vessel to ensure stable separation and protect the compressor from liquid carryover.
or Electronic level sensor with control valve
Reduces compression ratio per stage, improves efficiency, and increases system capacity for high-temperature heat pump and industrial systems.
Improve compressor reliability and system efficiency
Separates entrained oil from discharge gas and returns it to the compressor.
Hot, high-pressure gas with entrained oil mist enters tangentially.
Tangential entry creates a vortex that drives oil droplets to the wall.
Oil droplets collide with the coalescing pad, merge into larger drops, and drain downward.
Cleaned, oil-free gas exits the top and flows to the condenser.
Oil is returned to the compressor crankcase through a calibrated capillary (or orifice) for proper metering.
Collected oil accumulates in the sump.
At part load, system pressure differential decreases. The capillary restricts oil flow to match compressor oil consumption, preventing oil carryover and overfeeding.
Oil separator protects system efficiency and reliability by removing oil from discharge gas and returning it to the compressor.
Space above liquid level accommodates vapor during load changes and pump-down.
Maintains steady supply of liquid to the system. (Sight glass or float indicator)
Vapor space and internal volume provide storage for refrigerant during pump-down.
Extra volume assures adequate refrigerant inventory during normal operation and load changes.
Protects receiver from overpressure. Discharge to a safe location.
To liquid line / expansion valve (High-pressure liquid)
Ensures steady supply to expansion device
Store liquid refrigerant after the condenser — provide capacity, stability, and a clean supply to the system.
Removes and returns oil from discharge gas.
Oil return capillary To compressor crankcase
Keep oil out of the system and return it to the compressor.
Prevents liquid slugging; stores refrigerant and returns oil.
Protects compressor from liquid and ensures oil return.
Stores liquid refrigerant; provides supply and system protection.
Provides liquid inventory and a stable supply to the system.
Separates flash gas from liquid; supplies vapor for injection/economizing.
To injection port or economizer circuit
Improves efficiency and capacity control via vapor injection or subcooling.
Quote functional duty, not catalog nickname alone.
